Yeah. So this is the alert for the parked events that are parked in S3. So, as you remember, we have two different strategies to park events. So if there's an infrastructure issue, for example, an event cannot be delivered to the Lambda function, it's being parked in the dead letter queue provisioned by AWS.
However, if there's an issue within the code. Right? So if we process an event using a connector from the event store DB and it throws an error, it's within the code, so we need to park that event somewhere else.
So we park them in S3. This particular alarm... So it's a main Pro Ops connector parked events, and it's for the events parked in S3, so they were merged, I believe, one week ago. So we might still need some fine-tuning on the thresholds because every now and then we will park some events in those S3 buckets for various reasons, right? That we, of course, need to investigate because, for example, we might be replaying the events, and there might be a first name missing or an email missing or a phone number or whatever.
But we definitely need an action, fine-tuning on the thresholds.

I think there are other parked events that we can look into and see if these are actually valid. That's the triage that I'm talking about that we need to go into because right now, we've been setting up the alerting that we don't have, but we haven't gone through the alerting we do have.

content.text:
So Miha... That's the whole idea of the recording that we need to create on the playbook. It's to specifically walk through how to do exactly what Jennifer just said and have it be something where I can give it to JFFCO, I can give it to lads, and say, "Hey, follow this playbook." Here's a... That walks through how to run the playbook.
So we're working on this. Jennifer... I hear you. It's a bit stalled relative to some other priorities. But anyhow, are you clear on what Jennifer is looking for and the goal of that recording session and the playbook?

content.speaker: Michal Kawka
content.text:
Yeah, absolutely. So first of all, developers should have a clear understanding of how to investigate the alerts and the alarms. So what to do in case of an issue, what to look for, which dashboards to check, how to interpret them.
That's clear to me. The next priority is to configure alarms and alerts in such a way that they don't trigger unnecessarily because we've seen too much noise in the past, which made us ignore important stuff.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: That might be...

content.speaker: Michal Kawka
content.text: So we really need to get to a state where things trigger only when they are supposed to trigger.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
As my thinking is, the first point is more important than the second. They should be running in parallel. But the first point allows us to have more eyes on target, which gives us more ability to react and see things and have perspectives.
Then the other idea of tweaking and addressing them is the core thing that actually fixes the problem. But I think having more eyes on... If anything were to happen to me, how Jennifer, the team should be able to address this. That's my primary...
I think that's the most important thing to me.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text:
I get that being the primary, but until we get this to a net zero, it's very difficult to have anybody be... As far as outside of it,
we need to get everything taken care of, and we need to get the alarms to zero before we can tell people, "Hey, pay attention to the alarms because right now they're going off so much that no one has time to investigate those."

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Well.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text:
So we need... These are things that are still going off because of when the infrastructure was set up. We had the alerts not being thought about when we were setting stuff up, and we set up the alerts after the fact.
Then when we set up the alerts, nobody monitored them. So it's just been this cascading effect that started because when we set up all the CDK infrastructure at the beginning, we didn't set up alerts.
So we need to fix that, and we need to get to a place where we're not overwhelming anybody because if we say, "Now everybody can look at alarms," who's in charge of doing that? We need to have these tickets created and fixed so that we get to that net zero.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
That's a fair point. I'm just... I'm going to take your direction, I guess. Jennifer, here, that's going to be the priority then. So we'll clean up the playbook to where it currently is.
So let's close out the recording tasks. Me how? Then we'll pivot the effort for our NSRE to focus on cleaning up the events.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: True thing, yeah, awesome.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Okay, so we have four minutes left. Is there another grouping that we can knock off even by what it is?

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: I let me see, I don't know if there's another one that keeps going off, I have to look through the summaries.

content.speaker: francis.pena@llsa.com
content.text: I think I've seen another one about Cocky. Normally, I think that comes free. Yeah.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text:
The Cognito ones I have tickets in Jira, so at least they're alarming in Century as well. But that might be... So after we take care of these alarms... Wes, there's another task of trying to figure out what we look at in Century and what we look at in Cloudwatch.
Because right now, I think they're duplicated.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Yeah.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: But again, that's probably less so than getting these alarms. So assign an anomaly.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: I think the first priority is Century is probably misconfigured right now. The goal from states or just from my recollection is it's more about the intelligent notifications and insights that Centri can provide, as opposed to duplicating what CloudWatch is providing.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Makes sense. So, yeah. So, as I said, that's less so, but that would be the next thing once we get through these alarms.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Agreed.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text:
So, once we get through the ones that are always alerting, we will start noticing more of which ones are going off throughout the day.
Any that are noisy, we should hit and create tickets for those and see what's going on.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Sounds good.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text:
Find an anomaly. Looks like one that happens a lot. 2.
Okay, yeah, I would create one for this too, because it looks like this is happening a lot. So maybe this needs to be...

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: That is the signy of anomaly detection sign an anomaly.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: A different rate. Hope... Sorry.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: I got it.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Good boy.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
Okay, I think we're at the natural stopping point. I'll bid out build out the QE, maybe it's Q2 now, an epic to have that tracks all of these.
Francis, you have a couple of clear tickets there if you have bandwidth. You actually have a few things on your plate from Mandalor if you want to tackle those first. But I should have a ticket over to you at least around the SMS by end of date today, right, guys?

content.speaker: francis.pena@llsa.com
content.text: That's perfect.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Jennifer, thank you so much for walking us through and clarifying the urgency here for us.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Okay, yeah, no worries. Thank you, guys, for your...

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Guys, you...

content.speaker: Michal Kawka
content.text: Thank you so much, Thanke.
